title: Structure, Composition, and High-Field Superconductivity in Metal-Rich $\mathrmη$-Carbide-Type Compounds
authors: Manuele Balestra, KeYuan Ma, Harald O. Jeschke, Fabian O. von Rohr
abstract: $\mathrmη$-Carbide-type compounds have recently emerged as a diverse class of materials in the study of superconductivity. These phases contribute to a growing family of metal-rich quantum materials that exhibit unusual superconducting properties emerging from complex metallic bonding. Several members of the $\mathrmη$-carbide-type phases have been found to be bulk superconductors -- such as Nb$_4$Rh$_2$C$_{1-δ}$, Ta$_4$Rh$_2$C$_{1-δ}$, Ti$_4$Ir$_2$O$_{1-δ}$, and Ti$_4$Co$_2$O$_{1-δ}$ -- with transition temperatures up to $T_{\rm c} \approx$ 10 K and upper critical fields as high as $μ_0 H_{\rm c2}(0) \approx$ 30 T. Whereas the transition temperatures may fall within the range typical for intermetallic superconductors, the pronounced violation of the weak-coupling Pauli limit in many of these crystallographically high-symmetry materials is noteworthy. Here, we review recent progress on superconducting $\mathrmη$-carbide-type phases, emphasizing how crystal symmetry, synthetic challenges, transition-metal composition, and electronic structure govern their superconducting properties. Furthermore, we outline open questions and future directions, including the possible discovery of new $\mathrmη$-carbide-type materials.
summary: This review synthesizes research on $\eta$-carbide superconductors, highlighting their structural tunability and unusual superconducting properties. The core focus is explaining why their upper critical fields often exceed theoretical limits, pointing towards strong electronic correlations and symmetry-protected pairing mechanisms. The combination of experimental measurements and advanced DFT calculations provides a comprehensive picture of this fascinating class of quantum materials.
main_problem: The paper reviews the superconducting properties of $\eta$-carbide-type compounds, focusing on understanding the mechanisms behind their high upper critical fields ($H_{c2}$) which often violate the weak-coupling Pauli limit.
main_result: These materials exhibit superconductivity governed by complex electronic structures, and the large $H_{c2}$ values suggest unconventional pairing mechanisms, potentially involving strong spin-orbit coupling or multiband superconductivity.
method: The work combines systematic experimental characterization (transport, magnetization, specific heat) with advanced theoretical calculations, including DFT incorporating spin-orbit coupling (SOC) and $k_p$ theory.
model_or_system: The physical system is the $\eta$-carbide-type compound ($A_4B_2X$), a class of metal-rich quantum materials with high crystallographic symmetry ($Fd ar{3}m$). The electronic structure is complex, featuring multiple $d$ bands near the Fermi level.
key_observables: Superconducting transition temperature ($T_c$), upper critical field ($H_{c2}(0)$), specific-heat jump ($\Delta C/\gamma T_c$), and the effective electronic $g$-factor.
important_parameters: The Pauli paramagnetic limit ($\mu_0H_{	ext{Pauli}}$), crystal symmetry, and the degree of spin-orbit coupling (SOC).
main_assumptions: The analysis often assumes the applicability of BCS theory modified by strong coupling or that the observed $H_{c2}$ enhancement is due to SOC or FFLO states.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the crystal structure, map the chemical phase space of superconductors, and show $H_{c2}(T)$ curves, often comparing experimental data against the theoretical Pauli limit.
paper_structure: The paper follows a review structure, first introducing the material class and synthesis challenges, then detailing superconducting diagnostics ($T_c, H_{c2}$), and finally delving into theoretical explanations for the high critical fields using DFT and advanced pairing models.
why_interesting: The investigation into Pauli-limit violation and the role of SOC in determining $H_{c2}$ is highly relevant to understanding unconventional superconductivity, a key area in condensed matter physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
